titleStmt.title: Letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery to 'Canon', 10 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery (1844-1924) to 'Canon'. Originally a Liberal and a strong supporter of Gladstone, Montgomery was also a firm Unionist, but by 1916 he believed that Ulster Unionists had no choice but to accept Lloyd George's proposal for a six-county Northern Ireland. In the letter, Montgomery discusses the threat of Home Rule, stating that the six county scheme i.e. partition, should be accepted due to the situation what he was told by James Craig (1871-1940) and Edward Carson(1854-1935), Unionist leaders. Montgomery goes on to discuss how he feels it is a betrayal of the Protestants of Donegal, Monaghan and Cavan who signed the Solemn League and Covenant, however he feels they would be safer on the borders of a six county Ulster than an unstable nine county Ulster.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Copy 11th June, 1916. My dear Canon, I send you back your Republican newspaper, as you may
wish to keep it among your curiosities or give it to someone else. The present position about the exclusion of Ulster is
not pleasant; but in view of the state of things as revealed to
us privately by Sir Edward Carson and Colonel Craig, I believe
we ought if possible to profess our readiness to agree to the
six county scheme, and I believe that, though this would look
like a forsaking of our brother Govenanters in Monaghan, Cavan
and Donegall that they would really be safer on the borders of a
secure six county Ulster than inside the very insecure citadel
of a nine county Ulster: I say insecure because the Nationalists
would form too strong a body inside the nine counties, being
able as it is to return a majority of parliamentary representatives.
It appears to be a mad act of the Government at this moment to
propose to bring the Home Rule Act into operation for any part
of Ireland, and I hope that if we agree to acquiesce in the six
Counties scheme we shall make it very clear that we do not
approve, and take no responsibility for any such setting up
of Home Rule immediately after the Rebellion. Yours very truly,
